what_it_does: This markdown file is an upgrade addendum to a prior 'Sequenced State Progression' artefact; it extends the prior model with boundary-guided sequences, addressable incompletion, orphan-relation mechanics, saved-query sequence operators, interrogative resolution axes, anti-summary extraction rules, and UI/processing implications.
observations: Defines a sequence as an ordered, state-bearing traversal from a start boundary toward an end boundary, driven by resolving addressable incompletions.

Lists incompletion types as first-class structural states: proto-position, open seam, unknown node/property/relation, open socket, orphan relation, ghost target, branch point, ambiguity, unresolved step, undefined end boundary.
Distinguishes an orphan relation (deliberately created relation without a resolved target) from a ghost target (candidate target implied by a relation).
Requires explicit 'allow-orphan' state for orphan creation and prescribes metadata to record source, relation type, missing-target state, reason, actor, review state, and resolution channels.
Treats saved queries as sequence operators that retrieve unresolved positions and provide work surfaces for actor/tool/user blocks.
Frames who, what, when, where, how, why, and what-next as context-resolution operators generated from active incompletion.
States the 'store before next' rule: progression happens only after an answer is decomposed and structurally registered.
Separates mapping, design, and implementation as phases with distinct boundary conditions and progression criteria.
Mandates layered, schema-driven extraction for dense recordings and prohibits freeform summarisation as the primary output.
Replaces confidence with operational states (extracted, unresolved, ambiguous, candidate, requires review, user-confirmed, etc.) as the authority for progression.
Identifies speech operators in recordings ('and', 'or something else', repeated synonyms, long sentences, loops) as structural signals to preserve.
Requires self-application: the system should be able to process the recording that describes the extraction process itself.
Lists UI surfaces: structural map, sequence rail, context resolver panel, work queue surface, and event/state log.
Ends with an updated clean definition, law candidates, and an instruction to append or rewrite a full V2 artefact.
standouts: Addressable incompletion as the engine of sequence progression.
Orphan relations as intentionally created, queryable work surfaces.
Anti-summary rule requiring structured extraction against declared schemas.
Interrogative axes (who/what/when/where/how/why/what-next) as formal resolution operators.
Self-application as a practical bootstrapping test.
conventions: Uses front-matter metadata (title, articulation-state, substrate-position, authorship).
Heavy use of newly coined compound terms (e.g., 'addressable incompletion', 'orphan relation', 'ghost target').
Presents assertions as 'candidate laws' rather than proven rules.
Distinguishes storage primitive from chat/UI projection.
concerns: Many terms are introduced without formal definitions or examples, risking overlap (e.g., open socket vs orphan relation vs ghost target).
'Law candidates' are asserted without derivation, validation criteria, or falsifiability conditions.
The integration instruction is vague: 'append to the prior artefact or used to generate a full V2 rewrite'.
No concrete data model, schema, or test cases are provided.
Dismissing confidence entirely may conflict with practical needs for ranking or filtering candidate extractions.
notes: A dense design addendum that reframes sequence processing as boundary-guided traversal powered by first-class incompleteness, with strong prescriptions for extraction and UI.
questions: What is the exact data model for an 'addressable incompletion'?
How does the engine decide which incompletion to resolve next?
What is the declared schema for structured extraction outputs?
How are orphan relations reviewed, escalated, and eventually resolved?
What is the precise relationship between this file and the prior Sequenced State Progression artefact it upgrades?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it is an upgrade/addendum extending the prior artefact, and it does so by listing additions and an integration instruction.
apparent_themes: Sequence as state progression rather than a fixed workflow.
First-class representation of incompleteness and unresolved structure.
Structured extraction and reification over summarisation.
Operational state, not confidence, as the authority for next actions.
Self-application and bootstrapping of the processing system.
intention_signals: The file appears to be designing a sequence engine that can operate with incomplete knowledge, using unresolved structure itself to drive the next operation, and that can ingest dense source recordings into a structured substrate without collapsing them into summaries.
novelty: The treatment of orphan relations and incompletion as queryable first-class work surfaces; the formal use of interrogative pronouns as resolution operators; the anti-summary rule for dense recordings; and the explicit self-application requirement.
contribution: A set of design principles and candidate laws for boundary-driven, incompletion-driven sequence progression, plus rules for structured extraction of recordings and UI projection of sequence state.
summary_for_embedding: This markdown document is a V2 upgrade addendum to the Sequenced State Progression artefact, defining sequences as boundary-guided, state-bearing traversals driven by resolving addressable incompletions, and adding orphan-relation mechanics, saved-query operators, interrogative resolution axes, anti-summary extraction rules, and UI implications.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to sharpen the mechanism by which a sequence-processing system progresses through incomplete knowledge and extracts dense recordings into structured substrate.
